front: Figure: served as Governor of Arkansas before becoming the 42nd U.S. President, defeating incumbent George H.W. Bush in 1992
Event: as the young "Boy Governor" of Arkansas, was advised by a group nicknamed the "Three Beards"
Event: as governor, saw his approval rate plummet after enacting a highway-construction tax that disproportionately hit the heavier, older vehicles of the poor
Event: footage of Cuban refugees rioting at Fort Chaffee was used against this governor in an attack ad by Frank White, who ousted him from office for two years
Event: David Hale claimed this governor forced him to make a business loan
Event: as a Rhodes Scholar, had campaign debt paid off by James McDougal, with whom he partnered in a failed Ozarks real estate venture that developed into a scandal named for a creek
Event: played the saxophone on The Arsenio Hall Show during his successful 1992 presidential campaign
Event: used the campaign slogan "it's the economy, stupid" in defeating George H.W. Bush in 1992
Law: this president's administration presided over the mostly repeal of a 1933 banking law separating investment and commercial banks
Law: after promising to "end welfare as we have come to know it," worked with House Republicans led by Newt Gingrich to pass this reform
Law: this president's own health care reform initiative, which would have included a prescription drug benefit, ultimately failed to pass
Law: signed the Defense of Marriage Act and the Omnibus Crime Bill
Law: this president's administration adopted the "Don't Ask, Don't Tell" policy
Law: signed a 1993 ban on assault weapons that helped fuel the Republican Revolution of the 1994 midterms
Law: signed the North American Free Trade Agreement (NAFTA)
Law: spoke out against California's Proposition 187, comparing it to "bringing Big Brother into the schools"
Event: was advised on race issues by Vernon Jordan, former president of the National Urban League
Event: was criticized by civil rights activists for rushing home from a primary campaign stop to supervise the execution of a brain-damaged man named Ricky Ray Rector
Event: in a "Sister Souljah moment," criticized the rapper at a Rainbow Coalition speech over her remarks on the Rodney King riots
Event: was called "our first black president" by novelist Toni Morrison, at the height of the Paula Jones and Gennifer Flowers scandals
Event: amused comedian and civil rights activist Dick Gregory by making Newt Gingrich sit in the back of Air Force One
Event: this president's Cuba embargo was codified by the Helms-Burton Act after two planes operated by the exile group Brothers to the Rescue were shot down
Event: spies from the Wasp network known as the "Cuban Five" were arrested during this president's tenure
Law: an immigration surge prompted by Cuba's Special Period led this president to adopt this policy restricting immigration to those reaching US land
back: Wet foot, dry foot policy (Bill Clinton)

front: Event: ordered, through Attorney General Janet Reno, the raid that returned a Cuban boy named Elian Gonzalez to Cuba, seen as having hurt Al Gore's 2000 Florida campaign
Event: businessman Mansoor Ijaz ran off-the-books negotiations with Sudan on this president's behalf
Event: after the 1999 bombing of the Chinese Embassy in Belgrade during the Kosovo War, Chinese state media withheld broadcast of this president's official apology for four days
Event: in grand jury testimony about an affair with Monica Lewinsky, said "it depends on what the meaning of the word 'is' is"
Event: independent counsel Ken Starr investigated this president's affair with Monica Lewinsky and delivered the Starr Report to Congress
Event: this president was impeached by the House in 1998 on four counts including perjury and abuse of power, then acquitted by the Senate
Event: during this president's impeachment, prospective Speaker of the House Bob Livingston resigned his seat over information unearthed by Larry Flynt
Event: on his last day in office, this president's round of controversial pardons benefited commodities trader Marc Rich, founder of Glencore, who had donated over $400,000 to his library fund
Event: this president's last-day pardons scandal also benefited his own half-brother Roger
Event: this president's pardons scandal was investigated by U.S. attorneys Mary Jo White and James Comey
Event: this president's pardons scandal helped former Navajo tribal chairman Peter MacDonald and a lawyer who had funneled money for the Cali drug cartel
Event: this president defended his pardons scandal in his memoir My Life; the scandal also involved former Congressman Dan Rostenkowski
Event: Phil Hartman played this president in an SNL sketch stealing food from customers at a McDonald's
Event: this president escorted female students home from the library at Georgetown University, where he met the mother of comedian John Mulaney
Work: this president's lengthy 2004 autobiography was met with a rebuttal book, Because He Could, from his former advisor Dick Morris
